questions.question: What is the time complexity of a standard single-pass traversal to inspect or print every element in a 1D array of size N?
questions.explanation: To inspect each element in an array of size N exactly once, the loop must execute N times. Thus, the time complexity is linear, O(N).

questions.question: In a 0-indexed array of size N, what is the valid index range for elements?
questions.explanation: Because array indexing starts at 0, the first element is at index 0 and the N-th (last) element is at index N-1.

questions.question: Which of the following processes correctly describes reversing a 1D array in-place?

options.A: Allocating a new array of same size and copy elements backwards.
options.B: Using two pointers at the outer boundaries and swapping elements while moving towards the center.
options.C: Sorting the array in descending order using selection sort.
options.D: Repeatedly shifting all elements to the right by N steps.

questions.explanation: An in-place reversal does not use extra memory. By maintaining a left pointer (starting at 0) and a right pointer (starting at N-1), you swap their elements and increment/decrement the pointers until they meet in the middle.

questions.question: What is the auxiliary space complexity of reversing an array of size N in-place?
questions.explanation: An in-place algorithm modifies the input array directly without allocating extra space that scales with the input size. Only a few temporary pointer variables are needed, which takes O(1) auxiliary space.

questions.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of searching for an element in an unsorted array of size N using a linear scan?
questions.explanation: In an unsorted array, the target element could be at the very end or not present at all. In the worst case, the algorithm must check all N elements, leading to O(N) time complexity.

questions.question: If you access an array element at index 5 in a loop, how many memory locations are skipped relative to index 0?
questions.explanation: The offset calculation skips exactly 5 elements (index 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4) to directly address the 6th element at index 5.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of printing all elements of a square 2D matrix of dimensions N x N?
questions.explanation: A square matrix of dimensions N x N contains N^2 elements. Printing each element once requires traversing all of them, which yields a time complexity of O(N^2).

questions.question: Which array operation is characterized by copy-free, pointer-only reassignment in languages with automatic garbage collection?
questions.explanation: Assigning one array variable to another copy-assigns the reference pointer, meaning both variables point to the same array object in heap. This takes O(1) time and space.

questions.question: What is the result of applying a standard prefix sum operation to the array `[1, 2, 3, 4]`?
questions.explanation: A prefix sum array stores cumulative sums: `prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + arr[i]`. For `[1, 2, 3, 4]`, prefix sums are: 1, 1+2=3, 3+3=6, 6+4=10.

questions.question: Which traversal pattern scans a 2D array row-by-row?
questions.explanation: Row-major order traverses all columns of the first row, then all columns of the second row, and so on.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of searching an element in a sorted array of size N using binary search?
questions.explanation: Binary search repeatedly cuts the search domain in half, taking O(log N) operations to narrow down the target location.

questions.question: Which pointer operation moves a pointer to the next consecutive element in a C++ pointer array?
questions.explanation: Incrementing a pointer (`ptr++`) automatically increments its value by the size of the type it points to. Adding 1 to the pointer address achieves the same type-scaled jump.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of finding the maximum difference between any two elements in an unsorted array of size N?
questions.explanation: The maximum difference in an unsorted array is given by (MaxElement - MinElement). Both can be found in a single pass of O(N) operations.

questions.question: If you reverse an array of size N using an auxiliary array, what is the space complexity?
questions.explanation: Using an auxiliary array means allocating extra memory slots proportional to N, which yields O(N) auxiliary space complexity.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of copying all elements from one array of size N to another?
questions.explanation: Copying requires visiting all N elements and writing them to the new locations, which scales as linear time, O(N).

questions.question: Why does deleting an element at index k (where 0 <= k < N-1) in an array of size N require O(N) time complexity?

options.A: The element must be overwritten with garbage values.
options.B: All subsequent (N-1-k) elements must be shifted one position to the left to maintain contiguity.
options.C: The array's capacity is halved automatically by the OS runtime.
options.D: The memory block must be re-allocated and aligned.

questions.explanation: Arrays require contiguous storage. When an element is deleted, a hole is left in the memory layout. To keep the array contiguous, all elements situated to the right of index k must be shifted one slot to the left, which takes linear time on average.

questions.question: Why does iterating row-by-row (Row-Major traversal) through a 2D array typically run faster than column-by-column in languages like C/C++?

options.A: Row-major traversal uses fewer pointers in CPU registers.
options.B: Row-major layout matches how the array is stored sequentially in memory, maximizing CPU cache locality and hits.
options.C: Column-major traversal triggers OS page faults on every index operation.
options.D: C/C++ compilers disable loops optimizations for columns traversal.

questions.explanation: In C/C++, 2D arrays are stored row by row. Iterating row-by-row accesses memory addresses sequentially. This triggers CPU cache spatial locality, pre-loading adjacent elements into high-speed cache. Column-by-column traversal jumps addresses by row widths, causing cache misses.

questions.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of inserting an element into a dynamic array that has reached its capacity, assuming geometric resizing is used?
questions.explanation: When a dynamic array is full, the specific insertion that triggers a resize must allocate a new larger block of memory and copy all N existing elements to the new block. This makes that particular operation take O(N) worst-case time, even though the amortized cost remains O(1).

questions.question: Which of the following algorithm strategies is optimal to find a duplicate element in a read-only array of size N+1 containing integers from 1 to N, using O(1) extra space and O(N) time?

options.A: Sort the array and scan for duplicates.
options.B: Use a hash map to keep count of seen elements.
options.C: Floyd's Cycle Detection (Tortoise and Hare) algorithm by treating the array values as node pointers.
options.D: Iterative nested binary searches for each element.

questions.explanation: Since values are in the range 1 to N, we can view the array as a directed graph where index i links to index array[i]. The duplicate creates a cycle. Floyd's Cycle Detection finds the intersection of slow and fast pointers to identify the duplicate in O(N) time and O(1) auxiliary space.

questions.question: In a circular array implementation of size N, if your current read index is at i, which expression computes the index of the next element when moving forward?
questions.explanation: To wrap around to the beginning (index 0) after reaching the end (index N-1), we use modulo arithmetic: (i + 1) % N. When i = N-1, (N - 1 + 1) % N = 0.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of merging two sorted arrays of size N and M into a single sorted array?
questions.explanation: Using a two-pointer approach, we can compare elements of both arrays sequentially and write the smaller one to the output array. This requires traversing both arrays exactly once, taking linear time O(N + M).

questions.question: Which of the following array operations is commonly used to find the maximum sum of a contiguous subarray of size K?
questions.explanation: To find the maximum sum of a contiguous subarray of fixed size K, a sliding window maintains a running sum of K elements, adding the next element and subtracting the leftmost element as it slides, completing in O(N) time.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of Kadane's algorithm to find the maximum subarray sum in a 1D array of size N?
questions.explanation: Kadane's algorithm iterates through the array once, maintaining the maximum sum ending at the current position. It completes in a single pass, yielding O(N) time complexity.

questions.question: In C++, what is the function `std::rotate` used for?

options.B: To shift elements left or right such that the element at a given middle pointer becomes the first element.
options.C: To reverse the order of elements.
options.D: To sort the array in circular ascending order.

questions.explanation: `std::rotate` performs a left rotation, shifting elements so that the element at the specified pivot becomes the new first element, while the former head wraps to the tail.

questions.question: What is the space complexity of transposing an N x N matrix in-place?
questions.explanation: Transposing in-place only swaps elements across the main diagonal: `swap(matrix[i][j], matrix[j][i])` for `j > i`. This requires no extra arrays, taking O(1) auxiliary space.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of storing a sparse matrix using Coordinate List (COO) or Compressed Sparse Row (CSR) formats?

options.A: Allows faster random access by index than a standard 2D array.
options.B: Significantly reduces memory footprint by only storing non-zero values and their coordinate indices.

questions.explanation: Sparse matrices contain mostly zeroes. COO and CSR formats avoid storing these zeros, saving substantial memory and speeding up matrix multiplication on sparse datasets.

questions.question: In a 2D matrix representing an image, what operation is performed when a kernel filter matrix of size K x K is slid across the matrix to compute sum-products?
questions.explanation: Sliding a kernel filter and calculating cumulative sum-products at each position is the definition of a 2D convolution, which is widely used in image processing and CNNs.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the Dutch National Flag algorithm (3-way partitioning) to sort an array containing only 0s, 1s, and 2s?
questions.explanation: The Dutch National Flag algorithm uses three pointers to group elements in a single pass. Thus, the time complexity is linear, O(N).

questions.question: Which of the following problems can be resolved efficiently using the Two-Pointer approach on a sorted array?

options.A: Finding a pair of elements that sum up to a target value.
options.B: Finding the longest substring without repeating characters.
options.C: Determining if the array has duplicate values.

questions.explanation: On a sorted array, Two Pointers (left at start, right at end) can find pairs summing to a target in O(N) by shrinking bounds. Detecting duplicates is also trivial by comparing adjacent elements.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of updating a range sum query `update(L, R, val)` in an array of size N using a simple difference array?
questions.explanation: A difference array `D` allows updating a range by setting `D[L] += val` and `D[R+1] -= val`. This takes constant time, O(1), with the actual values rebuilt via prefix sum in O(N) later.

questions.question: Which approach rotates an array of size N to the right by k steps in O(N) time and O(1) auxiliary space?

options.A: Reverse the entire array, reverse the first k elements, then reverse the remaining N-k elements.
options.B: Shift all elements right by one step, repeating this k times.
options.C: Copy the last k elements to a temp array, shift the rest, and copy back.
options.D: Use a temporary stack to push elements and pop them into shifted positions.

questions.explanation: This is the classic three-step reversal algorithm. Reversing the entire array places elements roughly in their target regions. Reversing the first k and remaining N-k subsets restores their original relative order. It requires no extra space and runs in linear time.

questions.question: When traversing a large 2D grid/matrix using Breadth-First Search (BFS), why is it critical to mark cells as visited *when pushing* into the queue rather than *when popping*?

options.A: Marking on pop causes incorrect shortest path calculations.
options.B: Marking on pop allows cells to be pushed into the queue multiple times from different neighbors, leading to redundant work and potential memory exhaustion.
options.C: Marking on push enables faster bitwise address calculations.
options.D: Queue data structures in C++ throw exceptions if unvisited items are added.

questions.explanation: If a cell is not marked visited when pushed, adjacent cells can push that same cell into the queue repeatedly before it gets popped. In a grid, this causes exponential growth of duplicate entries in the queue, resulting in high memory overhead and redundant processing.

questions.question: If you perform an in-place array compaction (shifting all non-zero elements to the left and zeroing out the rest) in a single pass, what is the minimum pointer configuration needed?

options.A: One pointer tracking the current element.
options.B: Two pointers: one reading/scanning the array, and another tracking the write position for non-zero elements.
options.C: Three pointers: one reader, one writer, and one tracker for zeros.
options.D: It is mathematically impossible to compact an array in-place in a single pass.

questions.explanation: Using a two-pointer approach, the read pointer scans the array. Whenever a non-zero value is encountered, it is written to the position of the write pointer, and the write pointer is incremented. At the end, all indices from the write pointer to N-1 are filled with zeros.

questions.question: In multi-threaded array operations where threads write concurrently to adjacent indices of a shared array (e.g., thread 1 writes to index 0, thread 2 to index 1), what performance issue can arise on multi-core processors?

options.A: Memory leaks due to thread stack overlap.
options.B: False sharing (cache ping-ponging), where cores invalidate each other's cache lines because the adjacent indices reside in the same cache line.
options.C: Deadlock, as both threads compete for the array's base pointer lock.
options.D: Array structure corruption, as the base address shifts dynamically.

questions.explanation: False sharing occurs when threads on different cores modify variables residing on the same cache line (usually 64 bytes). Even though the threads write to independent array indices, the hardware must sync the entire cache line across cores, causing cache invalidations and severe performance degradation.

questions.question: For a three-dimensional array declared as A[L][M][N] stored in Row-Major order, what is the formula to calculate the offset of element A[x][y][z] from the base address, assuming each element occupies S bytes?

options.A: Offset = (x * M * N + y * N + z) * S
options.B: Offset = (x + y * L + z * M) * S
options.C: Offset = (x * L * M + y * M + z) * S
options.D: Offset = (x * M + y * N + z) * S

questions.explanation: To access A[x][y][z], you skip x 2D slices of size M * N, then skip y rows of size N within the current slice, and finally add index z. This yields the formula: (x * M * N + y * N + z) * S.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the most cache-efficient way to transpose a large matrix of dimensions 10000 x 10000 that does not fit in cache?

options.A: Naive transpose with nested loops scanning row by row.
options.B: Blocked (cache-oblivious) transposition, which divides the matrix into sub-matrices (blocks) that fit within cache and transposes block by block.
options.D: Converting the matrix to a coordinate list and sorting it.

questions.explanation: A naive transpose on large matrices leads to high cache miss rates. By dividing the matrix into small blocks (e.g. 64x64) that fit in cache, we ensure that elements read contiguously from one block are written to another block before their cache lines are evicted.

questions.question: When scanning a matrix spirally (outer boundary to inner center), which of the following details the most clean index boundary control strategy?

options.A: Recursive depth scanning checking coordinates history.
options.B: Using four boundary trackers (`rowStart`, `rowEnd`, `colStart`, `colEnd`) and adjusting them inward after completing each border traversal.
options.D: Using a DFS traversal marking visited cells in a separate matrix.

questions.explanation: Boundary tracking is the most efficient spiral matrix scan strategy. We maintain four boundary bounds. After traversing the top row, we increment `rowStart`. After the right column, we decrement `colEnd`, and so on, terminating when boundaries overlap.

questions.question: Which algorithm finds the longest contiguous subarray having an equal number of 0s and 1s in O(N) time and O(N) space?

options.B: Replacing 0s with -1s and using a Hash Map to store the first occurrence of each prefix sum.
options.C: Standard Sliding Window of size N/2

questions.explanation: By replacing 0s with -1s, the problem reduces to finding the longest subarray with a sum of 0. We compute prefix sums. If a prefix sum value is seen again at index `j` (originally seen at `i`), it means the subarray from `i+1` to `j` has a sum of 0. We track the first occurrence of each sum to compute lengths.

questions.question: What is the time and space complexity of querying the sum of any submatrix of size R x C in an N x M matrix in O(1) time after O(N * M) pre-processing?

options.A: O(N * M) query time, O(1) space
options.B: O(1) query time, O(N * M) auxiliary space for a 2D Prefix Sum array
options.C: O(R + C) query time, O(N + M) space
options.D: O(log(N * M)) query time, O(N * M) space

questions.explanation: By pre-computing a 2D prefix sum array where `prefix[i][j]` is the sum of the submatrix from (0,0) to (i,j), we can query any submatrix sum in constant time using the formula: `prefix[x2][y2] - prefix[x1-1][y2] - prefix[x2][y1-1] + prefix[x1-1][y1-1]`.

questions.question: In high-performance vector processing, what compiler loop transformation technique merges nested loops traversing 2D arrays to improve parallel execution efficiency?
questions.explanation: Loop collapsing merges nested loops (like rows and columns) into a single large 1D loop. This creates a larger iteration space, making it easier for the scheduler to distribute work evenly across multiple parallel threads or SIMD channels.

questions.question: If you are processing a data stream of size N and want to maintain the maximum element of all sliding windows of size K in O(N) time and O(K) auxiliary space, which data structure is optimal?
questions.explanation: A monotonic deque stores array indices in decreasing order of their corresponding values. For each new element, we remove indices of smaller elements from the back and indices outside the window from the front. The head of the deque always stores the index of the maximum element in O(1) amortized time per step.

questions.question: Which algorithm partitions an array of size N around a pivot in-place in O(N) time and O(1) space, but is unstable?

options.A: Hoare's Partitioning Scheme (used in Quick Sort)

questions.explanation: Both Hoare's and Lomuto's schemes partition an array in-place in linear time using pointers. However, because they swap elements across long distances without preserving their relative input order, both schemes are unstable.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the best known algorithm to search for a target value in a sorted 2D matrix of size N x M (where rows and columns are sorted)?
questions.explanation: By starting at the top-right corner (0, M-1), we can compare the cell to target. If cell > target, we move left (decrement column). If cell < target, we move down (increment row). This reduces search boundaries linearly, completing in O(N + M) worst-case time.

questions.question: Why is the classic 2D array matrix multiplication naive algorithm O(N^3) time complexity?

options.B: For each of the N^2 cells in the output matrix, we must perform a dot product of a row and column of length N (N operations).
options.C: It creates N^2 temporary matrices in memory.

questions.explanation: Matrix multiplication of two N x N matrices produces an N x N output. To calculate each of the N^2 elements, we iterate through N pairs of elements to compute their sum of products, leading to a total of N * N^2 = N^3 multiplications.

questions.question: Which matrix multiplication algorithm reduces the time complexity from O(N^3) to O(N^2.807) using divide and conquer?
questions.explanation: Strassen's algorithm uses algebraic formulas to compute the product of two 2x2 matrices using 7 multiplications instead of the standard 8. When applied recursively to blocks, it achieves O(N^log2(7)) = O(N^2.807) complexity.
